data.title: Yum China Announces Disclosure under Hong Kong Stock Exchange Rules in Relation to a Possible Quarterly Dividend
data.description:
SHANGHAI, April 16, 2025 /PRNewswire/ — Yum China Holdings, Inc. ( and , "Yum China" or the "Company") today announced, in compliance with the Rules Governing the Listing of Securities on The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited (the "HKEX") which require advance notice of board meetings at which a dividend is expected to be declared, that its board of directors (the "Board") will consider the declaration and payment of a quarterly dividend (the "Dividend"). If the Board decides to proceed, the declaration will be adopted by Board resolution on or around April 30, 2025 (Beijing/Hong Kong Time) and will be promptly disclosed by the Company.

The Company makes available through the Investor Relations section of its internet website at http://ir.yumchina.com its filings with the HKEX as soon as reasonably practicable after electronically filing such materials with the HKEX. These filings may also be obtained by visiting the HKEX's website at http://www.hkex.com.hk.

As no Board resolution in relation to the Dividend has been adopted as of the date of this press release, there is no assurance that the Dividend will be declared.

About Yum China Holdings, Inc.

Yum China is the largest restaurant company in China with a mission to make every life taste beautiful. The Company operates over 16,000 restaurants under six brands across around 2,200 cities in China. KFC and Pizza Hut are the leading brands in the quick-service and casual dining restaurant spaces in China, respectively. In addition, Yum China has also partnered with Lavazza to develop the Lavazza coffee concept in China. Little Sheep and Huang Ji Huang specialize in Chinese cuisine. Taco Bell offers innovative Mexican-inspired food. Yum China has a world-class, digitalized supply chain which includes an extensive network of logistics centers nationwide and an in-house supply chain management system. data.title: Logistri has taken possession of two properties in Gothenburg and is updating its current earnings capacity
data.description:
Logistri Fastighets AB (publ) ("Logistri") announces that the company has taken possession of two properties in Gothenburg on April 15. Logistri also communicates an updated current earnings capacity due to the now acquired properties. Profit from property management per share increased by 8% compared to the current earning capacity as of 1 January 2025 and amounted to SEK 12.7 (11.8) per share.

The properties that have been taken possession of are Göteborg Gamlestaden 74:2 and Härryda Solsten 1:137. The acquisitions have been financed with existing cash and senior bank debt. Logistri has expanded its banking relationships with another Swedish bank.

In the year-end report 2024, Logistri reported a current earning capacity, based on the property portfolio owned by the company as of January 1, 2025. After closing, the current earning capacity amounts to;

  • Revenues SEK 179.8 (158.9) million, an increase of 13%,
  • net operating income of SEK 166.0 (146.6) million, an increase corresponding to 13%,
  • profit from property management of SEK 93.2 (86.5) million, an increase corresponding to 8%
  • Profit from property management per share of SEK 12.7 (11.8), an increase of 8%

To read more about assumptions and a description of current earnings capacity, the company refers to the year-end report 2024.

"Logistri is strengthening its presence in Gothenburg through the acquisition of two modern light industrial properties in strategic, city-close locations. With continued strong demand and a limited supply of new construction, we see good opportunities for rent growth in the region. The properties comprise a total plot area of approximately 30,000 m², which creates attractive conditions for future value creation together with our tenants," says David Träff, CEO of Logistri.

For further information, please contact:

David Träff, CEO

Logistri fastighets AB (publ)

Phone: + 46 (0) 70 089 04 66

david.traff@logistri.se

About Logistri

Logistri Fastighets AB (publ) is a real estate company that invests in commercial properties primarily in the light industry, warehousing and logistics segments. The vision is to be a stable and long-term partner to companies that demand business-adapted and sustainable premises. The company's overall objective is to generate a stable cash flow and a high risk-adjusted return with high customer confidence. Our tenants are active in various industries, most of which are Swedish and international industrial and engineering companies. The properties are located in Stockholm, Gothenburg and in southern and central Sweden, in close proximity to strategic infrastructure such as major roads, railways and ports. The company is headquartered in Stockholm. The company's share was listed on the Spotlight Stock Market in 2017. Logistri is included in the MSCI Global Micro Cap index.

For more information regarding Logistri Fastighets AB, please visit www.logistri.se.

data.title: Singapore PM says little comfort from U.S. tariff postponement
data.description:
Singapore Prime Minister Lawrence Wong said on Wednesday there was little comfort in the U.S. postponing most of its proposed "reciprocal" tariffs, saying the changes had already created great uncertainty.

"The current baseline tariff rates are still much higher than what they used to be. And the changes have already created great uncertainty for businesses everywhere," Wong said at a lecture.

"No company can comfortably plan long-term investments while knowing that the tariff rates could be changed at a moment’s notice."

data.title: Independence Gold Intercepts High-Grade Gold at Larry Vein and Expands Mineralization at Multiple Targets Across 3Ts Project, BC
data.description:
Vancouver, British Columbia--(Newsfile Corp. - April 16, 2025) - Independence Gold Corp. ("Independence" or "the Company") is pleased to announce assay results from the winter drill program at its 100%-owned 3Ts Project, located in central British Columbia. Drilling continues to confirm the high-grade nature of the Larry Vein System and expand known mineralization across multiple target areas, including a new discovery at the Daisy target and deeper intercepts in the Ted-Mint Vein System. The 3Ts Project is located approximately 185 kilometres ("km") southwest of Prince George, British Columbia and is comprised of thirty-one mineral claims covering approximately 35,486 hectares ("ha") in the Nechako Plateau region of central British Columbia. The project is situated 16 km southwest of Artemis Gold Inc.'s Blackwater Mine and covers a low-sulphidation epithermal quartz- carbonate vein district within which nineteen individual mineralized veins, ranging from 50 m to more than 1,100 m in strike length and with true widths up to 32 m have been identified.

Figure 1: 3TS-25-10 and 3TS-25-13 Drill Collar Locations

To view an enhanced version of this graphic, please visit:

https://images.newsfilecorp.com/files/8610/248680_eeb6e439449d7bfb_002full.jpg

High-Grade Gold Intercept at Larry Vein System

Drill hole 3TS-25-10 targeted the Larry Vein System (approximately 145 metres ("m") east of the Tommy Vein System) and intersected a mineralized zone grading 5.95 grams per tonne ("g/t") gold and 23.09 g/t silver over 7.45m, including a high-grade interval of 1.86m grading 20.48 g/t gold and 80.11 g/t silver. This intercept was approximately 25m below the vein interval discovered in 3TS-25-09 and was designed to test for down-dip mineralization potential (see news release dated March 26, 2025). These results continue to demonstrate the potential of the Larry Vein to host high-grade, vein-hosted gold and silver mineralization near surface and at depth, and the potential of being included in the updated resource model (please see news release dated August 18th, 2022 for further details; "Technical Report on the Mineral Resource Estimate Update for the 3Ts Gold Project, Omineca Mining Division, British Columbia, Canada", authored by Armitage and Miller).

New Vein Discovery at Daisy Target and Deeper Ted-Mint Intercepts

Hole 3TS-25-13 tested the Daisy target area 230m east of the southern Ted-Mint Vein System and intersected previously unknown mineralized structures (see Table 1 below). Highlight intercepts from the Daisy veins include 2.85m grading 4.60 g/t gold and 94.74 g/t silver. These veins are interpreted as tensional offshoots located in the hanging wall of arcuate fault systems, reinforcing the Company's structural model developed from earlier discoveries at the Tommy, Ian, Johnny, and Larry Vein Systems. Veins associated with the Daisy Vein System can be traced on surface for 565m and are open along strike and at depth.

Hole 3TS-25-13 also intersected the Ted-Mint Vein at depth within a transitional zone between epithermal precious metals and deeper base metal mineralization. At a depth of 464.21 metres, the Ted-Mint Vein returned 3.54 metres grading 2.67 g/t gold and 26.55 g/t silver, immediately transitioning into a brecciated zone with base metal enrichment, including 5.25 metres with 0.12% lead and 0.20% zinc and anomalous copper up to 771 ppm. These results confirm the classic zonation pattern expected in low-sulphidation epithermal systems and will assist in defining the vertical and lateral extent of the Ted-Mint Vein System.

data.title: MGM China Seeks HK$23.4 Billion Revolving Credit Line
data.description:
MGM China is seeking a HK$23.4 billion, 60-month revolving credit facility with certain lenders, according to a Tuesday filing with the Hong Kong bourse.

The loan will have a fluctuating interest rate per annum based on the Hong Kong Inter-bank Offered Rate plus a margin ranging between 1.625% to 2.75%, depending on the casino operator's leverage ratio, the filing said.

Proceeds will be used to refinance MGM China's revolving credit facilities and for working capital, the filing said.

data.title: State fiscal aid is harder call in 2025 than 2020
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump’s tariff chaos is resuscitating a Covid-era support system. Like March 2020, markets are wildly fluctuating, and governments are discussing relief for companies at the sharp end of a big shock. But this crisis will prove trickier for politicians to navigate, not least because the tariffs themselves are a moving target.

Back in 2020, state interventions didn’t come cheap. The IMF estimated that the fiscal support measures announced as of September 11, 2020, were worth a total of $11.7 trillion – or nearly 12% of global GDP. Many advanced economies logged additional spending equivalent to more than 10% of their economic output during the pandemic.

This time around governments are not afraid of their healthcare systems being overwhelmed but are concerned about the knock-on effects of tariffs on critical industries and jobs. Canada, Portugal, South Korea and Spain have unveiled some sort of fiscal support package designed to help exposed industries manage the impact of tariffs. But the sums involved pale in comparison to their Covid-19 responses. Portugal last week said it had earmarked 10 billion euros ($11 billion) in aid for exporters, or slightly more than 3% of last year’s output of $303 billion. Spain is readying 14 billion euros for tariff-hit sectors. Meanwhile, Canada has made it easier for workers to claim unemployment benefits and enabled hard-hit firms can defer income tax payments – a move equal to 40 billion Canadian dollars in liquidity support.

How and when these support systems will be deployed depends on the outcome of negotiations with an erratic Trump administration. If the U.S. president digs his heels in and reinstates tariffs at their “Liberation Day” levels, some countries and export sectors face an existential threat. But this pain will be spread out. Researchers at Capital Economics reckon that if duties on most countries outside China stay at 10%, and retaliation from other nations remains limited, it will only shave off 0.4% of global GDP within two years.

But even if the damage is far more extreme than anticipated lawmakers may be reluctant to step in with the kind of generous schemes that were commonplace in 2020. That’s because they’re still paying the bill from the last crisis. Though debt in advanced economies had fallen back to 109% of GDP in 2024, from an all-time high of 122% in 2020, it's still above the pre-pandemic level of 103%.

Still, one advantage governments have in this budding crisis is time. The decision to impose lockdowns and cobble together worker relief systems was taken virtually overnight against the backdrop of an escalating public health crisis. This time around governments can spend months deciding how and when they will help different sectors. But even with this head start, the pain of the tariff crisis will unfold over many years.

Follow @jenjohn_ on X

CONTEXT NEWS

Spain and South Korea announced support measures, on April 3 and April 7 respectively, which are designed to prop up their economies in the face of the Trump administration’s new tariff regime.

Spain is prepared to offer its hardest-hit firms almost $16 billion – with economy minister Carlos Cuerpo urging other European countries to follow suit. On April 10, Portugal unveiled an $11 billion package of loans and other relief measures.

South Korean lawmakers have earmarked up to $68 billion to support businesses and financial markets.

U.S. President Donald Trump said on April 9 that he had authorised a 90-day tariff pause, with all countries except China now subject to a 10% levy.

data.title: Beyond Creatinine: First Real-World Evidence Highlights Proenkephalin A 119–159 (penKid) as a Valuable Kidney Function Biomarker
data.description:
• Real-World Implementation: Proenkephalin A 119-159 (penKid) has been successfully integrated into daily practice in intensive care unit (ICU), demonstrating its effectiveness in assessing kidney function and predicting acute kidney injury (AKI) in over 4,000 patients.

  • Superior Dynamics: PenKid augments traditional markers by detecting rapid changes in kidney function, particularly beneficial in critically ill patients where timely information is crucial.

  • Clinical Impact: PenKid proved valuable in identifying high-risk patients at ICU admission, especially those with a normal serum creatinine. Additionally, penKid provided valuable insights into kidney function during renal replacement therapy (RRT).

  • International Recognition: Beyond its use in reference hospitals in Germany, international experts, including those at the Mayo Clinic, acknowledge the added value of penKid on top of standard care.

HENNIGSDORF/BERLIN, GERMANY / ACCESS Newswire / April 16, 2025 / Diagnostic company SphingoTec GmbH ("SphingoTec") announces the publication of real-world data on the effectiveness of Proenkephalin A 119-159 (penKid) in improving kidney function assessment in intensive care units (ICUs) (1). This study confirms penKid's value in identifying high-risk patients at admission and supporting the management of renal replacement therapy (1). The published data complement a recent review from the Mayo Clinic that highlights penKid's potential as a new method to measure renal function beyond creatinine (2). Acute kidney injury (AKI) is a significant challenge in intensive care units (ICUs), affecting up to half of all patients and leading to increased morbidity and mortality (1). Traditional markers like serum creatinine have limitations, as they may not detect kidney dysfunction until significant damage has occurred. A recent study from the University Hospital Aachen marks the first publication based on real-world data from over 4,000 patients, reporting the outcome implementation of penKid in daily practice.

Study Overview

During a two-year period, the University Hospital Aachen, Germany implemented penKid in their ICU (1). The study analyzed almost 18,000 penKid measurements from 4,169 patients, including the use of a penKid-GFR formula to estimate glomerular filtration rate (GFR). PenKid outperformed traditional markers in assessing kidney function and remained dynamic under RRT, supporting clinical decision-making in critical care.

Key Findings

The data show that penKid allows for a significantly better assessment of renal dysfunction upon admission, particularly in identifying patients at risk of developing severe AKI. In patients with normal serum creatinine levels at admission, penKid and its GFR formula outperformed traditional methods in predicting AKI within 24 and 48 hours. As penKid is effectively removed by RRT, as demonstrated by studies such as Lorenzin et al. (3), persistently elevated levels during RRT may indicate ongoing renal dysfunction, while declining levels could signal renal recovery. This dynamic nature of penKid - even during ongoing RRT - can support decisions that may optimize RRT duration and reduce associated risks.

Clinical Significance

Prof. Dr. Gernot Marx, the Director of the Clinic for Operative Intensive Care and Intermediate Care at Uniklinik RWTH Aachen highlights, "After using penKid in clinical settings for five years, I see significant value in specific use cases due to its swift dynamics, addressing creatinine blind spots. The published data confirms our clinical observations and paves the way for bringing innovation into daily practice." Early detection of compromised renal function could allow for timely interventions, reducing the risk of emergency RRT and improving the chance for full renal recovery. In RRT scenarios, penKid could help de-escalate treatments and prevent unnecessary RRT, potentially reducing ICU stays.

Recent Review

The critical care community is seeking tools for improving kidney health assessment, as indicated by a recent review published by researchers from the Mayo Clinic (2). This review highlights penKid's ability in detecting rapid GFR changes and predicting AKI, particularly in critically ill patients. PenKid's strong correlation with measured GFR makes it a valuable tool, enabling more accurate and timely assessments of kidney function. The Mayo Clinic review also notes that penKid's ability to predict major adverse kidney events, such as worsening renal function and the need for RRT, aligns with its potential to provide valuable prognostic information in clinical settings.

##

References

  • Martin, L. et al. Implementation and One-Year Evaluation of Proenkephalin A in Critical Care . Int. J. Mol. Sci . 2025 , 26 , 2602. https://doi.org/10.3390/ijms26062602

  • Sheikh MS, Kashani KB. Beyond creatinine: New methods to measure renal function? Eur J Intern Med. 2025 Jan 31:S0953-6205(25)00025-1. doi: 10.1016/j.ejim.2025.01.015.

  • Lorenzin, A. et al. Human Proenkephalin A 119-159 (penKid) in Extracorporeal Therapies: Ex vivo Sieving Coefficient, Diffusive Clearance, and Hemoadsorption Kinetics. Blood Purif. 2024, 53, 773-780. doi: 10.1159/000540061.

About SphingoTec

SphingoTec GmbH ("SphingoTec"; Hennigsdorf near Berlin, Germany) is a diagnostic company focusing on the out-licensing of innovative critical care biomarkers for diagnosing, predicting, and monitoring acute medical conditions. SphingoTec develops its biomarkers to the commercial stage and partners with IVD companies to make them available on different IVD platforms. SphingoTec's proprietary biomarker portfolio includes Proenkephalin A 119-159 (penKid), a biomarker for the assessment of kidney function in critical diseases, commercially available on diagnostic platforms AFIAS and Nexus IB10 and bioactive Adrenomedullin 1-52 (bio-ADM), a biomarker for the assessment of endothelial function in conditions like sepsis. Discover more on www.sphingotec.com

Head of Marketing and Communication

SphingoTec GmbH

Phone +49-3302-20565-0

Email: press@sphingotec.com

data.title: Long Gold Takes Over as Most Crowded Trade in April: BofA SurveyMarket Talk
data.description: 'Long gold' ends a 24-month streak for 'long Magnificent 7' as the most crowded trade, Bank of America's April global fund manager survey shows. 'Long gold' is cited as the most crowded trade by 49% of investors surveyed by BofA, followed by 24% mentioning 'long Magnificent 7', a reference to Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Nvidia, Meta and Tesla stocks. Gold is also expected to be the best performing asset in 2025 according to 42% of investors, up from 23% in March. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0824 GMT - 'Long gold' ends a 24-month streak for 'long Magnificent 7' as the most crowded trade, Bank of America's April global fund manager survey shows. 'Long gold' is cited as the most crowded trade by 49% of investors surveyed by BofA, followed by 24% mentioning 'long Magnificent 7', a reference to Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Nvidia, Meta and Tesla stocks. Gold is also expected to be the best performing asset in 2025 according to 42% of investors, up from 23% in March. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0822 GMT - Fund managers have slashed their allocation to U.S. equities by a record amount over the last two months, Bank of America's global fund manager survey for April shows. "In just 2 months, U.S. equity allocation has been slashed by a record 53 percentage points," BofA says in a press release accompanying the survey. In April, investors lower their U.S. equity allocations to a net 36% underweight, the most underweight since May 2023. The survey also shows a record number of global investors want to cut their allocation to U.S. equities. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0817 GMT - ASML Holding's reiteration that the Dutch semiconductor-equipment maker will grow in 2025 and 2026 is a positive sign amid uncertainty from President Trump's fast-changing tariff decisions, Jefferies analysts write in a research note. The company said it was working to minimize the impact of any tariffs on its systems and component shipments as much as possible, the analysts note. ASML forecasts between 30 billion euros and 35 billion euros in sales this year. ASML shares trade 6.1% lower after first-quarter orders of 3.94 billion euros came in below a Visible Alpha consensus of 4.84 billion euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0817 GMT - Hong Kong shares ended lower, weighed by tech and auto stocks. The benchmark Hang Seng Index declined 1.9% to 21056.98 and the Hang Seng Tech Index fell 3.7%. China's 1Q growth beat market expectations, but investors are concerned that the impact of U.S. tariffs could hit the Chinese economy as early as 2Q. Meituan led losses, down 8.1%. JD.com lost 5.5%. Geely Automobile and Li Auto fell 5.5% and 3.9%, respectively. Meanwhile, property stocks led gains, with Longfor Group up 1.9%. China Resources Land and China Overseas Land & Investment each advanced 1.9%. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0812 GMT - Sartorius's better-than-expected 1Q results and a quantified 2025 outlook reassure investors, reflecting a solid market recovery, Jefferies analysts say in a note. The German life-science company's issued 2025 guidance of 6% sales growth and underlying Ebitda margin of around 29%-30%, which should be well-received by investors, they say. Shares are up 3.2% at 200.50 euros. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0810 GMT - Gold futures rise to a new record high, with investors fleeing to safe-haven assets as Asian equity markets follow U.S. stock futures lower. The benchmark Hang Seng Index was down 2.5% at midday in Hong Kong, with the market waiting for uncertainty around U.S. President Trump's trade policies to clear up. The U.S. Commerce Department said it has initiated probes into the national security effects of importing semiconductors and pharmaceutical imports. The safe-haven demand for gold from the prospect of a wider trade war was accentuated by Chinese purchases, where gold-backed ETFs are picking up in demand, ANZ Research analysts say in a note. Futures are up 2.3% at $3,315.90 a troy ounce, having peaked at $3,317.80/oz earlier in the session. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0804 GMT - Investors turn negative on the outlook for U.S. corporate profits, Bank of America's April global fund manager survey says. A net 28% of responding investors say that the outlook for U.S. profits is unfavorable, the survey says. This is the highest print for this indicator since November 2007. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0756 GMT - Bets on a weaker dollar rose to their highest level in nearly 19 years in April, according to the latest Bank of America global fund manager survey. Some 61% of investors expect a weaker dollar in the next 12 months, the most since May 2006. The survey also showed 69% of investors see the dollar as overvalued, up 10 percentage points from the previous month and the highest since December 2022. The survey comes in the wake of President Trump's decision to impose sweeping tariffs against U.S. trading partners. This has weighed on the dollar due to concerns about the impact on U.S. economic growth. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0754 GMT - Heineken delivered another quarter of positive signs in key growth engines such as Vietnam, Mexico, Brazil and India, Jefferies analysts Edward Mundy and Andrei Andon-Ionita say in a note. The Dutch brewer is currently at an early stage of a multi-year turnaround program made to deliver profitable growth and improving free cash flow, they say. Heineken backed its guidance for 2025 for growth in both volume and revenue, and net profit growth in line with operating profit growth. On the negative side, Heineken unperformed in Europe, but this is likely to improve into the second quarter and face easier comparatives in the third quarter, they add. "As confidence in Heineken's compounding potential increases, shares should re-rate," the analysts add. Shares are up 2.1% at 76.74 euros.(michael.susin@wsj.com)

0751 GMT - Italy's opinion on whether to use its so-called golden powers to allow UniCredit's takeover of smaller peer Banco BPM could come earlier than expected and with conditions, Equita says in a research note after two Italian newspapers reported the decision could land in the next few days. A conditional approval is reportedly likely, with limits on branch reductions and on the rationalization of investment banking activities, guarantees on its headquarters' location and a potential exit from Russia. "These elements do not, in our opinion, diminish the industrial sense of the deal; however, it will be necessary to understand their magnitude in detail in order to properly assess the impact on the generation of synergies and value creation from the transaction," analyst Andrea Lisi writes. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0747 GMT - Global equity allocation is at the lowest level in 21 months, the Bank of America's global fund managers survey for April shows. Allocation to global equities stood at a net 17% underweight in April, the most underweight since July 2023, BofA says. Allocation to stocks has declined 52 percentage points since February, marking the biggest 2-month drop since April 2020. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0743 GMT - The euro could rise further if the latest eurozone balance of payments data add to the story of a rotation out of U.S. equities into Europe, ING analyst Chris Turner says in a note. The data could show investments in non-eurozone equities by eurozone residents fell if they are repatriating, they say. It could also show foreigners bought more eurozone equities if they are increasing their exposure to Europe, helped by fiscal stimulus plans in the region. "If the above turns out to be true, investors should have a little more faith in the euro-dollar rally." The euro rises 0.7% to $1.1364 and ING expects it to test $1.15. The data are due at 0800 GMT. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Asian Stock Markets Fall on Tariff Concerns2nd Update
data.description:
By Dow Jones Newswires Staff

Asian equity markets declined on Wednesday as concerns about tariffs again weighed on sentiment, with technology shares hit by uncertainty over how the Trump administration's trade policies will affect supply chains.

Japan's Nikkei Stock Average shed 1.0%, with chip and electronics shares leading losses. Advantest dropped 6.5% and Renesas Electronics lost 4.2% after semiconductor giant ASML's first-quarter orders disappointed.

Chip and electronics stocks dragged on South Korea's Kospi, too, with the benchmark index ending down 1.2%. The after-hours drop in shares of AI-chip giant Nvidia weighed on local chip-making stocks, with SK Hynix, which supplies high-bandwidth-memory chips to Nvidia, dropping 3.7%.

Index heavyweight Samsung Electronics lost 3.4%, while carmaker Hyundai Motor slid 2.8% amid lingering U.S. tariff concerns.

The downturn in chip and tech stocks came after Nvidia said that it anticipates a charge of up to $5.5 billion tied to exporting its artificial-intelligence chips to China.

"The Street will take this news with clear nervousness worried these are the first shots fired in the tech battle between the U.S. and China," analysts from Wedbush Securities wrote in a note. "Beijing/Xi are not just going to take this news and walk away."

Chinese shares closed mixed as markets digested first-quarter gross domestic product data, which was better than expected, though it did little to diminish worries about the tariff hit the economy faces. More global banks cut growth forecasts for China after the data release, including Goldman Sachs and Nomura.

The Shanghai Composite Index rose 0.3%, but the Shenzhen Composite Index fell 1.1% and the ChiNext Price Index lost 1.2%.

The Hang Seng Index closed 1.9% lower and the Hang Seng Tech Index declined 3.7%.

In another sign of market nerves, spot gold surpassed $3,300 an ounce to hit a new intraday high.

Asian currencies were mixed, with the safe-haven yen strengthening around 0.7% to 142.30 to the U.S. dollar. The dollar edged 0.1% higher to 4.4170 versus the Malaysian ringgit, while the Australian dollar was steady at 0.6346 against its U.S. counterpart.

Trepidation over tit-for-tat tariffs remained at the front of investors' minds after Trump said China will need to come to the U.S. if it wants to negotiate for lower tariffs.

"The ball is in China's court," the president said in a statement Tuesday. "China needs to make a deal with us, we don't have to make a deal with them."

The Trump administration has temporarily paused tariffs on some consumer electronics, giving investors a brief reprieve.

"While recent tariff exemptions suggested some flexibility, the latest signals from Washington point to a tougher trade posture, a shift that may strengthen safe-haven demand," said Konstantinos Chrysikos at Kudotrade.

Write to Kimberley Kao at kimberley.kao@wsj.com

data.title: Eurozone Current Account Surplus Declines in February
data.description:

The consensus estimate for the month stood at 37.3 billion euros.

In the 12 months to February, the bloc's current account surplus totaled 411 billion euros, against 299 billion euros a year earlier.

data.title: Bulgaria's Balkan Property Instruments raises 5.3 mln euro in cap hike
data.description:
SOFIA (Bulgaria), April 16 (SeeNews) - Bulgarian real estate investment trust (REIT) Balkan Property Instruments [BUL:BPI] said it has raised some 10.46 million levs ($6.1 million/5.3 million euro) in a capital increase through a new share issue.

Warrant holders subscribed for 951,000 new company shares between April 1 and April 14, Balkan Property Instruments said in a stock exchange filing on Monday.

The company offered to its warrant holders up to some 4.5 million new shares with a par value of 10 levs apiece at an issue price of 11 levs each.

Balkan Property Instruments issued in December a total of some 4.5 million freely transferrable warrants, each giving their owner the right to subscribe for one company share at a price of 11 levs in a future capital increase within five years, according to an earlier statement.

Shares in the company last traded on April 7 when they closed at 9.50 levs on the BaSE market of the Bulgarian Stock Exchange, bourse data show.

(1 euro = 1.95583 levs)

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0817 GMT - ASML Holding's reiteration that the Dutch semiconductor-equipment maker will grow in 2025 and 2026 is a positive sign amid uncertainty from President Trump's fast-changing tariff decisions, Jefferies analysts write in a research note. The company said it was working to minimize the impact of any tariffs on its systems and component shipments as much as possible, the analysts note. ASML forecasts between 30 billion euros and 35 billion euros in sales this year. ASML shares trade 6.1% lower after first-quarter orders of 3.94 billion euros came in below a Visible Alpha consensus of 4.84 billion euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0636 GMT - ASML Holding's guidance for the current quarter is below market expectations, Citi analysts write in a research note. The Dutch semiconductor-equipment maker expects sales between 7.2 billion and 7.7 billion euros and a gross margin between 50% and 53%, a range it said was larger than usual due to uncertainty around tariffs. The sales guidance midpoint of 7.45 billion euros is below consensus of 7.77 billion euros, the analysts note. Meanwhile, the gross margin midpoint is about 91 basis points below consensus of 52.4%, they add. For the year, ASML continues to expect between 30 billion and 35 billion euros in sales. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0616 GMT - ASML Holding is grappling with uncertainty stemming from U.S. President Trump's fast-changing tariff decisions, Citi analysts write in a note to clients. "Tariff uncertainty is clearly increasing and clouding the outlook," they say. The Dutch semiconductor-equipment maker backed its 2025 targets, but Chief Executive Christophe Fouquet said uncertainty with some customers could lead the company to the lower end of its sales guidance range. ASML is forecasting between 30 billion and 35 billion euros in sales this year. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0601 GMT - Delivery Hero will likely see growth in all regions, excluding Asia, for its first quarter, Citi analysts write in a note. The U.S. bank expects the German food-delivery company to report 8% growth in gross merchandise value, ahead of consensus of 7%. As a result, Citi upgrades its stock recommendation to neutral from sell. It cuts its target price to 23.5 euros from 26 euros to reflect foreign exchange rate fluctuations and headwinds related to the share price of Middle East subsidiary Talabat. Shares closed at 23.44 euros on Tuesday.(najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0139 GMT - UOB KayHian cuts its year-end KLCI target to 1620 from 1690, citing persistent U.S.-China tariff tensions, high U.S. bond yields, and potential "Trump tantrums" that weigh on valuations and recession risks. Although Trump rolled back extreme tariff threats, escalating trade frictions may still dampen sentiment, analysts say in a note. They remain defensive but see near-term trading opportunities. Tech remains vulnerable, but relief on phones, PCs, and chips opens tactical entry points in oversold names like Inari Amertron and NationGate, they say. Malaysia's lower tariff exposure also supports trade diversion, likely benefiting electronic manufacturing services players, UOB KH adds. Top picks include Eco World Development, Gamuda, Hartalega, and Hong Leong Bank. The KLCI is flat at 1486.60. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2312 GMT - The Trump Administration's restrictions on Nvidia's exports of AI H20 chips to China looks to be part of a more comprehensive approach that we will learn about from the government soon, DA Davidson analyst Gil Luria tells The Wall Street Journal. Chip makers Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing, Broadcom, Intel and Qualcomm down between about 2% to 4% in post-market trading. "The administration is focused on national security and it's a matter on how much is sold to China," Luria says. "The Chinese government is trying to beat the U.S. government and Chinese companies are trying to beat American companies." Nvidia would rather deal with tariffs than selling restrictions, Luria says, adding that around 20% to 30% of Nvidia's sales come from China or Chinese companies. Nvidia down 5.6% after-hours to $105.90. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1800 GMT - Software companies are expected to face a slowdown in activity from enterprises as they seek to preserve cash in the current environment, BofA Securities analysts say in a research note. While tariffs pose little direct impact to the software industry, with the exception of eCommerce companies such as Shopify, the cyclical nature of software project approvals represents a threat, the analysts say. BofA lowers its 2Q and 2025 estimates 1% and 4% across the software industry. With the software group off 18% from February 18, the analysts say they are also lowering price targets on stocks. "While positive tariff-related news flow could drive a step-change recovery of multiples (as seen following the 90-day tariff pause headline), we believe it's prudent to adjust price objectives based on the environment we are in today." (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1453 GMT - Rent the Runway said economic uncertainty from tariffs, as well as the timing of customer retention improvements, could yield different full-year financial results compared with expectations. The clothing rental platform declined to share revenue guidance for the year due to uncertainty. First-quarter revenue has been affected negatively by a lower subscriber base at the start of the period, as well as higher spending on inventory, executive say. Rent the Runway made its largest ever inventory investment to try to offer higher end clothes and give customers the sense they are getting a better value from renting rather than buying. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1444 GMT - Rent the Runway ended its 4Q with a 5% decline in active subscribers as revenue from subscription and reserve rental revenue fell 1.2% from the prior year. The apparel rental company said it had lower subscription acquisition because it spent less on advertising to try to understand the effectiveness of its marketing spending. Now, Rent the Runway is trying to increase its inventory to grow subscriptions and improve customer satisfaction. It made its largest ever inventory acquisition, which it expects to double inventory in fiscal 2025. Shares fall 10% to $4.85. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

0920 GMT - The outlook for Chinese companies supplying power to AI-related businesses is positive, following partial exemption from U.S. reciprocal tariffs, according to Daiwa analysts Dennis Ip and Leo Lu in a research note. While there remains some controversy over whether production capacities in China are included in the latest exemption lists, the analysts believe Shenzhen Megmeet Electrical, and Jiangsu Azure stand to benefit from this development, as both companies maintain overseas production capacities in Southeast Asia. In addition, they anticipate positive market sentiment for Shenzhen Envicool Technology, given its 15-20% revenue exposure to the U.S. market, despite its lack of production capacity in Southeast Asia. The analysts reaffirm positive rating on China's AI power sector. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0846 GMT - Xiaomi is positioned to be among the top ten automakers globally by 2035, Bernstein analysts write in a note. The company has seen significant success in China with its first model, SU7. Xiaomi's first EV model has seen strong demand, record production ramp-up and impressive gross margins at 20.4%. Bernstein forecasts Xiaomi to achieve around 405,000 unit deliveries this year, surpassing the company's guidance of around 350,000 units. By 2035, the company can reach around 3.5 million units, ranking it among the top ten automakers globally. The next model launch, YU7, for mid-2025, would be the next positive stock catalyst, it says. Bernstein initiates its coverage of Xiaomi with an outperform rating and sets the target price at HK$55.00. Shares last traded at HK$43.10. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0839 GMT - Telecom Italia's sale of its submarine cable unit Sparkle looks positive, ING analyst Jan Frederik Slijkerman writes in a note. TIM--as Telecom Italia is known--agreed to sell Sparkle to the Italian government and fibre operator Retelit in a 700 million euro deal. This move will allow the telecom company to focus on other business areas, Slijkerman says. Shares are up 0.6% at 32 European cents. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0812 GMT - Sartorius's better-than-expected 1Q results and a quantified 2025 outlook reassure investors, reflecting a solid market recovery, Jefferies analysts say in a note. The German life-science company's issued 2025 guidance of 6% sales growth and underlying Ebitda margin of around 29%-30%, which should be well-received by investors, they say. Shares are up 3.2% at 200.50 euros. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0657 GMT - Praram 9 Hospital likely has high earnings growth with potential upside, Maybank Securities (Thailand)'s Nontapat Sahakitpinyo says in a research report. The Thailand hospital operator should deliver decent earnings in 1Q, supported by strong international revenue, the analyst says. Its 2Q momentum should remain robust on a yearly and quarterly basis, underpinned by Middle East patient inflows post-Ramadan, the analyst says. A tax benefit of THB15 million is also expected in 2Q or 3Q from medical equipment donations to public hospitals, the analyst adds. The brokerage has a buy rating and a target price of THB32.00 on the shares, which are 3.6% higher at THB23.30. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0655 GMT - Ipsen's first-quarter sales came in ahead of consensus expectations after a better-than-expected result for its Somatuline cancer drug, RBC Capital Markets analysts say in a research note. The drug's sales were robust, benefiting from continuing shortage of generics competitors, and beat expectations by 14%, according to RBC. "Clearly, it is likely that supply improves through the year and thus sales could still move into a decline in [the second half], but this is a good start," the analysts say. The outlook for Somatuline and potential impacts from U.S. tariffs are likely to be in focus at the company's earnings call, RBC says. Ipsen reiterated its full-year guidance. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0139 GMT - UOB KayHian cuts its year-end KLCI target to 1620 from 1690, citing persistent U.S.-China tariff tensions, high U.S. bond yields, and potential "Trump tantrums" that weigh on valuations and recession risks. Although Trump rolled back extreme tariff threats, escalating trade frictions may still dampen sentiment, analysts say in a note. They remain defensive but see near-term trading opportunities. Tech remains vulnerable, but relief on phones, PCs, and chips opens tactical entry points in oversold names like Inari Amertron and NationGate, they say. Malaysia's lower tariff exposure also supports trade diversion, likely benefiting electronic manufacturing services players, UOB KH adds. Top picks include Eco World Development, Gamuda, Hartalega, and Hong Leong Bank. The KLCI is flat at 1486.60. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1852 GMT - Johnson & Johnson's 1Q report bodes well for the medical-technology sector, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note, citing the company's positive commentary on overall demand and preliminary thoughts on tariffs. J&J's sales of medical devices came in slightly below expectations; however, management highlighted a normalized procedure environment, no recession signaling in the near-term as elective procedure growth remains steady and an expectation for sales to grow in the back half of the year. The company expects a $400 million hit from tariffs, though the analysts note that J&J has some of the highest exposure to China in the medical-technology sector, at about 10%. "Overall, we view the totality of today's updates as a positive for the rest of MedTech earnings," the analysts write. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0955 GMT - A U.S. investigation into pharmaceutical imports and potential tariffs in the coming months will likely limit clarity from pharma companies during their 1Q results, Barclays analysts say in a note. The investigation, initiated April 1, aims to assess the national security implications of pharmaceutical imports, including finished generics, nongenerics and active pharmaceutical ingredients, and has to be finalized in around nine months. "With Pharma having been excluded from the initial reciprocal tariffs and it being our house view that some sort of Commerce Department investigation being required for these sectoral tariffs, this isn't too surprising but at least provides some timing framing," they say. Barclays analysts estimate that AstraZeneca, Roche and Grifols appear most insulated, whereas ​GSK and Sandoz look most exposed to a potential tariff risk. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0751 GMT - Italy's opinion on whether to use its so-called golden powers to allow UniCredit's takeover of smaller peer Banco BPM could come earlier than expected and with conditions, Equita says in a research note after two Italian newspapers reported the decision could land in the next few days. A conditional approval is reportedly likely, with limits on branch reductions and on the rationalization of investment banking activities, guarantees on its headquarters' location and a potential exit from Russia. "These elements do not, in our opinion, diminish the industrial sense of the deal; however, it will be necessary to understand their magnitude in detail in order to properly assess the impact on the generation of synergies and value creation from the transaction," analyst Andrea Lisi writes. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0704 GMT - EQT shares could see an initial positive reaction to strong comments around BPEA fundraising, especially given the high short interest, JPMorgan analysts write. However, the outlook on exits, fund valuations and fundraising could potentially drive consensus EPS downgrades. "Hence we remain cautious." The company had a strong first quarter, but the outlook points to slowdown. EQT's fee-generating assets under management rose to 142 billion euros, 3% above consensus on stronger fundraising of 12.4 billion euros. This was driven by the activation of BPEA IX fund earlier than expected, JPMorgan says. EQT now expects a "material slowdown" in exit activity, its listed holdings have declined by 9% quarter-to-date, and industry-wide fundraising volumes are not expected to return to 2021 levels until 2027. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0641 GMT - Nordea reported another solid result with pre-provision profits coming in 4% ahead of consensus, Jefferies analyst Alexander Demetriou writes. A positive was the 2% beat on net interest income, with volumes coming in better than expected, he says. Fees were below while costs were better. Pretax profit came in 7% ahead and the CET1 ratio of 15.7% was 20 basis points ahead, he says. Nordea continues to target a return on equity of above 15% in 2025 with consensus currently at 14.5%. Jefferies rates Nordea at buy with a 13 euro price target. Shares closed at 11.15 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0139 GMT - UOB KayHian cuts its year-end KLCI target to 1620 from 1690, citing persistent U.S.-China tariff tensions, high U.S. bond yields, and potential "Trump tantrums" that weigh on valuations and recession risks. Although Trump rolled back extreme tariff threats, escalating trade frictions may still dampen sentiment, analysts say in a note. They remain defensive but see near-term trading opportunities. Tech remains vulnerable, but relief on phones, PCs, and chips opens tactical entry points in oversold names like Inari Amertron and NationGate, they say. Malaysia's lower tariff exposure also supports trade diversion, likely benefiting electronic manufacturing services players, UOB KH adds. Top picks include Eco World Development, Gamuda, Hartalega, and Hong Leong Bank. The KLCI is flat at 1486.60. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1829 GMT - U.S. cyber insurance renewal premium rates, which have fallen year-over-year for the last three quarters, are expected to remain under pressure near-term, barring a significant event, Fitch Ratings says. "The cyber insurance line was profitable for 2024, but direct written premiums fell for the second straight year due to declining rates and fewer policies in force," according to Fitch. Ongoing challenges include intensifying market competition, as well as "an evolving claims environment increasingly influenced by technological changes, including artificial intelligence, rather than regulations or case law," Fitch adds. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1636 GMT - U.S. beverages and household and personal care companies will report challenging 1Q results, Citi analysts say in a research note. The companies aren't only facing a difficult set-up for the sector with soft commentary on the U.S. and Europe, but also from key emerging markets such as Mexico and India. Both markets had been positive elements of growth for the companies in the past few quarters. Many haven't provided commentary on tariffs, so that will be a large focus. Citi has a cautious near-term view on Procter & Gamble, Colgate-Palmolive and Coca-Cola, and a positive view on Church & Dwight, Boston Beer and Keurig Dr Pepper. Citi cuts its target price for the stocks of Estee Lauder, Newell Brands, Clorox and Brown-Forman, and raises its target price on Boston Beer, Monster Beverage and Kenvue. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1553 GMT - Bank of America expects the Trump administration's agenda to result in fewer or more refined regulations, as well as a smaller federal government that will mean fewer regulatory inquiries and related matters. Executives on an earnings call say that sometimes regulations get in the way and overshoot the issues they oversee, but that they're looking forward to having them come back to the middle. They add that they look forward to seeing more relief as the administration's nominees get into position. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1533 GMT - Bank of America stuck to a familiar formula when calculating its loan loss reserves. The bank's provision for credit losses of $1.5 billion in 1Q25 was flat with provisions from 4Q24. Executives on an earnings call said as they have in prior quarters, they came to their figure using a model involving blue chip economic indicators which they feel is more independent. Using those indicators as a baseline, they also planned for four scenarios - three of which were downside scenarios. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1431 GMT - Bank of America isn't forecasting a recession just yet, but says its research team has lowered its GDP growth rates for 2025. The team also doesn't see any rate cuts this year, CEO Brian Moynihan says on an analyst call. Despite plenty of market uncertainty, Bank of America says consumers are still spending--either by debit/credit cards, checks, Zelle payments or other forms. Moynihan says that while consumers may be changing their spending behavior, in the aggregate they "keep pushing money into the economy." (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1326 GMT - Citigroup reports a jump in 1Q revenue with the aid of a 14% hike in investment banking fees. Total investment banking revenue increased 12% to $1 billion, driven by higher fees in its advisory business, partially offset by declines in equity capital markets and debt capital markets. Advisory fees were up 84% as the business gained wallet share, both overall and across numerous sectors, Citigroup says. The equity capital markets saw a 26% drop in fees as demand dried up for follow-ons and convertibles, while fees in the debt capital markets business were down 3% against a strong prior-year performance. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1304 GMT - Citigroup reports operating costs of $13.4 billion, down 5% from the same quarter a year ago thanks in part to a drop in compensation costs. The company says this came from favorable foreign currency translation, productivity savings from an organizational simplification effort, a reduction in stranded costs and lower severance. Those were partially offset by more technology and communications spending, higher professional fees and higher advertising and marketing costs, Citigroup says. Citigroup up 1.5% premarket. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1210 GMT - Recent geopolitical volatility is set to affect transaction activity and fundraising of European alternative brokers and asset managers, leaving some names more exposed than others given their foreign exchange and fundraising profiles, Citi says in a research note. Compared with traditional peers, alternatives are more diversified, continue to outgrow the rest of European financials, and have significant deployable assets, the analysts say. Citi remains constructive on the growth potential of the sector in the medium term but downgrades Partners Group and Antin from buy to neutral, joining EQT. Citi prefers Intermediate Capital Group, CVC Capital Partners and Bridgepoint. "While short-term share-price outlook is less certain, in the long term, we continue to see [alternatives] as a key overweight in financials," they write. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0741 GMT - Allocation to bonds shows a record increase, according to Bank of America's April global fund manager survey. Relative to history, investors are overweight bonds, but also to utilities, staples and U.K. stocks, BofA says in an accompanying press statement. The rush to bonds is caused by U.S. capital flight and global fiscal panic. Alongside the record increase in allocation to bonds, global equity allocation fell to its lowest since July 2023. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0009 GMT - Oil prices edge higher in the early Asian session on possible position adjustments. However, dimming prospects of trade deals amid the global tariff conflict may weigh. Negotiations between the EU and the U.S. have made little progress toward a deal, ANZ Research analysts say in a research report. Also, "Trump administration officials have indicated that the 20% reciprocal tariff, as well as other tariffs targeting sectors such as cars and metals, will not be removed outright," the analysts add. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are up 0.2% at $61.47/bbl; front-month Brent crude oil futures are 0.2% higher at $64.82/bbl. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1905 GMT - Oil futures tread water for much of the session and settle lower as markets appear to steady after two weeks of tariff-driven volatility. "Macro sentiment has finally stabilized, which has helped stabilize oil as well. Crude will likely tie closer to the fundamental landscape," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. Future demand remains at the forefront of trader concerns, with the IEA cutting its 2025 forecast to around 730,000 barrels a day from just over 1 million b/d, following demand downgrades by OPEC and the U.S. EIA. Meanwhile, the Keystone pipeline that carries Canadian oil to the U.S. Midwest restarted following repairs after last week's spill, the Pipeline Safety and Hazardous Materials Administration said. WTI settles down 0.3% at $61.33 a barrel and Brent falls 0.3% to $64.67 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1342 GMT - Crude futures are lower after the IEA cuts its 2025 demand growth estimate by around 300,000 barrels a day, citing an expected negative impact of U.S. tariffs on global economic growth. The downgrade to 726,000 b/d follows forecast reductions by OPEC and the U.S. EIA. The lower demand outlooks coincide with OPEC+ gradually unwinding production cuts, including plans to bring back 411,000 barrels a day in May. The IEA also reduced its 2025 supply growth estimate by 260,000 b/d to 1.2 million b/d on lower expected U.S. and Venezuelan output, and says the OPEC+ May increase could be a lot smaller given overproduction by some members. WTI is off 0.3% at $61.36 a barrel, and Brent is off 0.3% at $64.70 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1019 GMT - TotalEnergies should deliver first-quarter adjusted net income of $4.27 billion, RBC Capital Markets analysts Biraj Borkhataria and Adnan Dhanani write. Ahead of the French energy major's update, the analysts had expected $4.67 billion. Earnings in its upstream division are still seen at $2.4 billion but its integrated LNG division should report $1.37 billion, down from a prior forecast of $1.53 billion, they write. Refining and chemicals' projected earnings are now $329 million from $423 million, while integrated power is forecast to deliver $488 million from earlier expectations of $637 million, they write. Shares rise 1% to 50.75 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0903 GMT - BP's higher tax rate will drag on its first-quarter earnings, Jefferies analysts Giacomo Romeo and Kai Ye Loh write as they lower their earnings expectations. They cut their estimate for replacement cost net income by 6% to $1.68 billion and their replacement cost EBIT estimate by 1% to $4.57 billion. The British energy major said last week that it now expects a 50% tax rate, above the 43% market watchers had expected. Its post-earnings conference call is expected to focus on buyback sustainability when oil is priced around $60 a barrel and capital expenditure flexibility during a low oil-price environment and divestments, they write. Shares trade up 1.4% at 345.85 pence.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)
